text: Presenting a video on use of adrenal venous sampling for the management of primary aldosteronism by robotic adenectomy for left adrenal microadenoma. Primary aldosteronism is the commonest cause of secondary arterial hypertension. Adrenal venous sampling is an essential diagnostic step in the most patients to distinguish between the unilateral and the bilateral We herein report a case of primary hyperadosteronism with the use of adenal venous sampling for localization of site of origin. A forty-eight-year-old hypertensive and diabetic male presented with incidental left adenal mass with hypokalemia during evolution for the left arm fracture. investigation showed a hypokalemia with the serum potassium of two point five millimole per liter city showed a rounded median lip nodule measuring one cross point nine centimeter in size with the absolute washout ratio of ninety two percent in the left adrenal gland mri showed the possibility of lipid poor adenoma and morning serum aldosterone was six thirty five nanogram per dl and the plasma rain activity was two a plasma aldosterone to a run-in ratio was three one seven point
The video shows adrenal venous sampling with cannulation of the right adrenal vein, followed by the same procedure on the left adrenal vein.

The patient is positioned in the left renal position for a left adrenalectomy. Pneumoperitoneum is created, and standard left robotic ports are placed with an anti-clockwise cranial shift. The da Vinci robotic system is docked, and the surgeon begins deflecting the left colon medially towards the upper pole.
The surgeon dissects the upper pole of the left adrenal gland free from the undersurface of the spleen, dividing the lienorenal ligament to facilitate exposure.

The surgeon identifies the left renal vein and clips the adrenal vein before dividing it. The adrenal gland is separated from the upper pole of the kidney using monopolar cautery, achieving hemostasis. The gland is further dissected away from the posterior abdominal wall and surrounding structures, with intervening vessels clipped using Hem-o-lok clips and divided. The adrenal gland specimen is then retrieved.

Interoperating finding where a consult time was twenty one minutes and blood loss was minimal. Postoperative course was uneventful. He was discharged on post-operative day three with serum potassium of four point five millimole per liter and osteopathologist showed a left adenal adenoma. Conclusion, adenal venous sampling may help a disappear cause of hypokalemia along with the localization of the side of adenoma. Here we present a case with the adenoma of one centimeter size that also served to emphasize the importance of adenal venous sampling as a useful tool for localization of a lesion in case of ambiguous adenal masses. This has also opened a new subset of the patient that may be offered a minimal invasive adenectomy along with the curative treatment of hypokalemia.
